etymology.text:
Compare فَاحَ (fāḥa), فَاخَ (fāḵa), نَفَحَ (nafaḥa), نَفَخَ (nafaḵa), فَحَّ (faḥḥa).

Borrowed from Aramaic פחא / ܦܚܐ (paḥḥā, “trap, snare, gin”).

body.text: : (S, A, L, Msb, * K:) said to be a Pers., or foreign, word, arabicized: (TA:) [from the Pers.
:] AM says that the Arabs called it
signifies the same: (L:) pl. of the former
Such a one leaped from the snare of Iblees

entries.lexicon_en: Al-Ṣāḥib bin ʿAbbād, Al-Muḥīṭ fī l-Lugha (d. c. 995 CE)
entries.lexicon_ar: المحيط في اللغة للصاحب بن عباد
entries.body_html: فخ الفخيخ دون الغطيط في النوم. والأفعى تفخ. وافتخ الرجل في النوم. والفخ مصيدة. وهو من القسي ما لان. والفخة القذرة من النساء. ونوم الغداة. والرائحة، فخت الرائحة مثل فاحت. والفخخ استرخاء في الرجلين.

entries.lexicon_en: Supplément aux dictionnaires arabes by Reinhart Dozy (d. 1883 CE)
entries.body_html:
فخ: فخ: (أنظر لين) ويجمع على أفخاخ أيضا (همبرت ص76، بوشر) سراك، أجولة، آلة يصاد به الطيور والسباع.
فخ: مكمن، موضع يكمن فيه أي يتواري فيه ويستخفي - (بوشر، همبرت ص140).
فخة، والجمع فخاخ: فخ، شراك، أحبولة، لصيد الطيور (ألكالا) وفيه الجمع فخا وهو من خطأ الطباعة والصواب فخاخ.

entries.lexicon_en: Arabic-English Lexicon by Edward William Lane (d. 1876)
entries.lexicon_ar: المعجم العربي الإنجليزي لإدوارد وليام لين
entries.body_html:
فخ

1 فَخَّ, aor. ـِ inf. n. فَخِيخٌ (S, K) and فَخٌّ; and so ↓ افتخّ, (K, TA,) inf. n. اِفْتِخَاخٌ; (TA;) said of one sleeping, He snored; or made a sound in breathing, audible by persons around him; syn. غَطَّ: (S, L, K:) or فَخٌّ in sleep is [the making a sound] less than what is termed غَطِيطٌ [inf. n. of غَطَّ]: and it signifies also a man's sleeping, and blowing in sleep. (L.) 8 إِفْتَخَ3َ see the preceding paragraph.

فَخٌّ A snare, trap, gin, or net, for catching game or any kind of wild animals or birds; syn. مِصْيَدَةٌ: (S, A, L, Msb, * K:) said to be a Pers\., or foreign, word, arabicized: (TA:) [from the Pers\. فَخْ:] AM says that the Arabs called it طَرْقٌ [q. v.; and see also طِبْقٌ]: (TA:) and ↓ فَخَّةٌ signifies the same: (L:) pl. of the former فِخَاخٌ (S, A, Msb, K) and فُخُوخٌ. (S, A, K.) وَثَبَ فُلَانٌ مِنْ فَخِّ إِبْلِيسَ (tropical:) [lit. Such a one leaped from the snare of Iblees] means such a one repented. (A.) فَخَّةٌ A sleep in which the sleeper snores, or makes a sound in breathing audible by persons around him: (S: [see 1:]) or a sleep in which the sleeper blows: (L:) or a sleep after coïtus: (K:) or a sleep in which the sleeper rests on the back of his head, (IAar, L, K,) and blows by reason of satiety: (IAar, L:) or a sleep in the early part of the morning or of the forenoon, between the time of the prayer of daybreak and sunrise or after sunrise: and, when tired. (A.) One says, هُوَ يَنَامُ الفَخَّةَ [He sleeps the sleep termed فَخَّة]. (A.) A2: See also فَخٌّ.
